title: Authentication & Authorization
objectives: Clearly separate authentication (who you are) from authorization (what you can do)
Understand the three authentication factors and multi-factor auth (MFA)
Choose between sessions and tokens for your app
Pick the right authorization model — RBAC, ABAC, or ACL
Answer authN/authZ questions confidently in interviews

sections.markdown:
**Imagine going through an international airport.**

1. At the immigration counter, an officer scans your **passport** and matches your face. They're checking *who you are*. That's **authentication**.
2. Then you approach the **boarding gate**, which scans your **boarding pass**. It confirms this specific person is allowed on *this specific flight*, in *this specific seat*. That's **authorization**.
3. Your passport doesn't get you on the plane — the boarding pass does. And your boarding pass without a passport gets you nowhere.

**One identity — many permissions.** You can enter a country with your passport (auth) but you still can't just walk into any airplane cockpit (authz).

Every login-protected feature you'll ever build is exactly this pattern.

**Authentication (AuthN)** = proving *who you are*.
**Authorization (AuthZ)** = deciding *what you're allowed to do*.

They are two distinct steps, always in that order. Never conflate them.

## The three authentication factors

Strong authentication uses at least two of these — that's **multi-factor auth (MFA)**.

rows: Your phone (OTP), a hardware key (YubiKey), a smart card

sections.markdown: **Location** and **behavior** are sometimes called weak fourth factors. Banking apps often use them silently — 'you logged in from a new city, please verify.'

## Sessions vs tokens — the modern choice

After someone logs in, how does the server 'remember' them across requests? Two mainstream approaches, and they have very different trade-offs.

rows: Needs a shared session store across servers
Any server can verify with a public key
Instant — delete the row in Redis
Hard — you have to add a blocklist or wait for expiry
APIs, mobile apps, microservices, single sign-on

sections.markdown: **The trade-off in one line:** sessions are *stateful, easy to revoke, hard to scale*. Tokens are *stateless, easy to scale, hard to revoke*. Everything else is detail.

## Authorization models — RBAC vs ABAC vs ACL

Once you know *who* someone is, how do you decide *what* they can do? Three models cover 95% of real systems.

rows: Every user has one or more roles; permissions attach to roles
Rules that evaluate on any attribute of user, resource, time, location
'allow if user.dept == doc.dept and time is business hours'
Each resource has an explicit list: which users can do what

sections.markdown:
## A complete sample program — login, session, permission check

This Dart program simulates the full flow in memory. No framework — pure primitives so you can see every step:
## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1 — the classic: 'the client will check the role.'**

Any check that happens only in the client can be bypassed by a determined user (edit the JavaScript, use a proxy, call the API directly with `curl`). **Authorization must happen on the server, on every request.** The client can hide UI, but the server must refuse the action.

**Mistake 2: Storing passwords in plaintext (or with MD5/SHA-1).**

If your database is stolen — which happens all the time — every password is exposed. Even fast hashes like SHA-256 can be brute-forced billions of times per second on GPUs. Use **bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2** — slow, salted hashes designed for this.

**Mistake 3: Confusing 'logged in' with 'allowed.'**

A logged-in user is not automatically allowed to do anything. The classic bug (BOLA / IDOR): `GET /orders/42` returns *any* order because the server only checked authentication, not *whether this user owns order 42*. This is the #1 API bug in the wild.
## Best practices
**1. Hash passwords with bcrypt or argon2** — never plain, never MD5, never SHA-256 alone.
**2. Enforce authorization on the server, on every request.** Don't rely on hidden UI.
**3. Follow the principle of least privilege.** Give users (and services) exactly the permissions they need, no more.
**4. Enable MFA for anything that matters.** SMS OTP is weakest, TOTP apps (Google Authenticator, Authy) are much better, hardware keys (YubiKey, WebAuthn) are best.
**5. Log every auth event — login success, login failure, permission denial — with a request ID.** You'll thank yourself during an incident.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — bcrypt round-trip.** Hash a password with bcrypt (any language). Show that the same password produces a *different* hash each time (because of salting). Then verify.

**Why:** Salting mystifies most beginners. Seeing 'same input, different outputs, but verify still works' clicks fast.

**Practice 2 (Medium) — Role-protected routes.** Build a small HTTP server with `/public`, `/user`, `/admin` endpoints. `/public` is open; the others require a session with the right role.

**Why:** That's the shape of every real web app.

**Practice 3 (Hard) — Ownership check (fix a BOLA).** Extend Practice 2: `GET /orders/:id` should only return the order if `session.userId == order.ownerId`. Log the denial when it fails.

**Why:** That single check prevents 90% of API auth bugs found in the wild.

**Practice 4 (Expert) — TOTP verifier.** Implement TOTP (RFC 6238) yourself: given a shared secret, generate the current 6-digit code and verify one from Google Authenticator.

**Why:** MFA feels magical until you've built it. Then it's just HMAC + a 30-second window.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What's the difference between authentication and authorization?**

**A:** **Authentication** proves *who you are* — validating a password, an OTP, a token, or a biometric. **Authorization** decides *what you're allowed to do* — checking whether this authenticated user has permission for this specific action or resource. Always in that order: first you authenticate, then you authorize. A common pitfall is doing only one — a user who is logged in but not authorized to access a specific record is exactly the BOLA (broken object-level authorization) vulnerability that tops the OWASP API Top 10.

**Q2: When would you choose server sessions vs JWTs?**

**A:** **Server sessions** if you value **easy revocation** and don't mind a shared session store — great for traditional web apps where 'log out this user everywhere right now' matters. **JWTs (or opaque tokens)** if you value **stateless scale** — no server-side state, so any of your API servers can verify the token with a public key. JWTs are the norm for mobile apps, single-page apps, and microservices, but revocation is harder — you need short expiry + refresh, or a blocklist in Redis. Real systems often combine them: short-lived access tokens (JWTs) + a refresh token backed by a revocable server-side session.

**Q3: How should passwords be stored, and why not SHA-256?**

**A:** With a slow, salted, memory-hard hash designed for passwords — **bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2**. SHA-256 is fast: a modern GPU can compute billions per second, making brute force cheap. Bcrypt takes ~100 ms per hash *deliberately* — slow enough that offline brute force becomes economically infeasible. **Salting** (a random per-password value) means two users with the same password get different hashes, so a stolen database can't be attacked with precomputed rainbow tables. Use the library defaults; don't invent your own.

**Q4: RBAC vs ABAC — how do you choose?**

**A:** **RBAC (Role-Based)** attaches permissions to roles and roles to users — simple, auditable, works for most B2B apps ('admins can do this, editors can do that'). **ABAC (Attribute-Based)** evaluates rules over attributes at request time — user attributes, resource attributes, time of day, location. It's more powerful ('allow if user.department == doc.department AND doc.classification <= user.clearance') but harder to reason about and audit. Start with RBAC. Add ABAC only when you have genuinely context-sensitive rules that roles can't express.

**Q5: What is MFA, and why isn't SMS OTP good enough?**

**A:** MFA (multi-factor authentication) requires two or more distinct factors — something you know, have, or are. It defeats stolen-password attacks: even if a password leaks, the attacker still needs the second factor. **SMS OTP** is a weak second factor because SIM swapping is real: attackers social-engineer telecom operators into moving your number to their SIM, then intercept the OTP. **TOTP apps** (Google Authenticator, Authy) are much better — the seed never leaves your phone. **WebAuthn / FIDO2 hardware keys** (YubiKey, Face ID as a passkey) are best — cryptographically bound to the specific site, immune to phishing.

**Q6: What is the principle of least privilege?**

**A:** Every user and every service should have **exactly the permissions it needs to do its job — nothing more.** A read-only reporting service shouldn't have write access. A cashier account shouldn't be able to delete transactions. A microservice shouldn't have root DB credentials. The principle limits the blast radius when (not if) something is compromised. In practice: prefer specific roles over 'admin', rotate keys, review permissions periodically, and log privileged actions.

**Q7: What is BOLA / IDOR and how do you prevent it?**

**A:** BOLA (Broken Object-Level Authorization) — also called IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) — is when the server acts on a client-supplied resource ID without checking that the requester owns or can access that resource. Example: `GET /orders/42` returns any order because the code only checked 'is the user logged in.' It doesn't check 'does this user own order 42.' The fix is boring but essential: on **every** endpoint that touches a resource, verify ownership or permission on the server. It's the #1 API vulnerability year after year on the OWASP API Top 10.

## Real-world examples

- **GitHub, Slack, Google, Microsoft** all expose OAuth so third-party apps can act on a user's behalf with scoped permissions. Clicking 'Authorize' is you consenting to specific scopes.
- **Banking apps** enforce step-up MFA — a fresh biometric or OTP before a big transfer, on top of your login session.
- **Passwordless auth** (magic-link email or WebAuthn passkeys) is quietly winning — the password is the weakest link, so remove it.
- **The OWASP API Top 10** is dominated by BOLA and other authorization bugs, year after year. Every senior interview eventually asks about this list.

## Advanced notes

- **OAuth 2.0 vs OpenID Connect** — OAuth is about *authorization delegation*; OIDC layers *identity* on top with an ID token. 'Sign in with Google' is really OIDC.
- **PASETO** — a JWT alternative with simpler, safer defaults. Growing adoption in security-conscious teams.
- **Session fixation** — an attacker gives a victim a session ID, then hijacks the session after the victim logs in. Prevention: regenerate the session ID on login.
- **CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery)** — an attacker's site tricks a logged-in user's browser into making a state-changing request. Prevention: SameSite cookies + CSRF tokens on state-changing endpoints.
- **Zero-trust architecture** — never assume network location means trust; authenticate + authorize *every* request, even internal ones. This is where big infra teams are heading.

flashcards.back: AuthN proves who you are. AuthZ decides what you can do. Always AuthN → AuthZ.
Something you know (password), something you have (phone/YubiKey), something you are (fingerprint).
Sessions: stateful, easy to revoke, hard to scale. Tokens: stateless, easy to scale, hard to revoke.
Salted slow hash (bcrypt/scrypt/argon2). Never plain, never MD5/SHA-256 alone.
Server acts on a client-supplied resource ID without checking ownership. The #1 API bug.

quiz.explain: First prove who you are, then decide what you can do.

quiz.q: Best way to store a password?
quiz.explain: Slow, salted, memory-hard hashes designed for passwords.

options: On the server, on every request
quiz.explain: Never trust the client with authorization decisions.

quiz.q: SMS OTP is weaker than TOTP apps because…
quiz.explain: SIM-swap is the standard bypass; TOTP seeds never leave your phone.

options: The server trusts a client-supplied resource ID without ownership check
quiz.explain: '/orders/42' returns any order — the classic bug.

challenges.text: Hash and verify a password with bcrypt. Show that hashing the same password twice yields different hashes (salt), but verify still returns true.
Build a lookup table: 5 users, each with roles like 'user' or 'admin'. Print which users can access 'admin-only' vs 'anyone' endpoints.
Small HTTP server with `/public`, `/user`, `/admin` — session cookie + role check on each.
Add password-change flow: require the current password before setting a new one; re-hash and store.
Add ownership check to `/orders/:id` so a logged-in user can only see orders they created. Log every denial.
Implement TOTP (RFC 6238) — same algorithm as Google Authenticator. Verify a real 6-digit code from the app.
Build session-fixation protection: regenerate the session ID on login and invalidate the old one.

interviewQuestions: What's the difference between authentication and authorization?
When would you choose server sessions vs JWTs?
How should passwords be stored, and why not SHA-256?
RBAC vs ABAC — how do you choose?
What is MFA, and why isn't SMS OTP good enough?
What is the principle of least privilege?
What is BOLA / IDOR and how do you prevent it?
What is session fixation and how do you defend against it?
